content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
August 25,
1993
Samuel Brunelli
Executive Director
American Legislative Exchange Council
214 Massachusetts Avenue,
N.E.
Washington,
D.C.
20002
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
I-93--170a
Dear Mr. Brunelli:
This is in response to your request for clarification of our
advice concerning the issues raised in the Leonard Advice
Letter,
No.
1-93—170,
which was issued on June 3,
1993.
Both the
first Leonard Advice Letter and your most recent letter are
seeking general advice with respect to the gift provisions of the
Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
Thus,
we are treating your
request as one for informal assistance.
2
OUESTION
Does the status of the American Legislative Exchange Council
(“ALEC”)
as a nonprofit charitable organization pursuant to
Section 501(c) (3)
of the Internal Revenue Code exempt travel and
lodging provided by the organization to Members of the California
Legislature from the gift limitations?
CONCLUSION
The status of the organization as a charitable organization
does not alone exempt travel and lodging provided by the
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
I—93—170a
Page
2
organization from the gift limitations.
Each expenditure must be
separately analyzed in order to determine if the requirements of
the “ethics” provisions are satisfied.
However, if ALEC is a charitable or religious organization
organized under 501(c) (3)
of the Internal Revenue Code and the
actual transportation and related lodging and subsistence is
reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose,
or to
an issue of state, national,
or international public policy,
travel and lodging provided to the Members of the California
Legislature do not constitute gifts subject to the ift limits.
FACTS
The facts are substantially the same as in the first advice
request.
ALEC is a nonprofit voluntary membership organization of
state legislators and private members organized under Section
501(c) (3)
of the Internal Revenue Code.
The primary purpose of
the organization is to educate legislators and private sector
members on the issues that affect state government and to develop
solutions to those issues.
The organization engages in research
and discussion regarding public issues and accomplishes its
educational goals by publishing its findings and sponsoring
educational seminars and conferences.
Making speeches is normally
not one of the activities organized by ALEC.
ALEC, at its
own discretion,
reimburses Legislative members
who attend ALEC seminars and conferences to defray the costs of
travel and lodging.
In your request for further assistance,
you have clarified
that ALEC is a charitable and educational nonprofit organization
under Section 501(c) (3)
of the Internal Revenue Code.
You have
asked how this information may change the analysis used in the
initial Leonard Advice Letter,
supra.
ANALYSIS
In the Leonard Advice Letter,
supra,
we analyzed whether
private sector donors,
or ALEC,
would be the source of a gift of
travel and lodging to a Members of the California Legislature
where they are reimbursed from private sector donations.
Based on
the facts provided,
we concluded that the source of the gift would
be the nonprofit organization.
We also concluded that the gifts were subject to the $270
gift limit,
assuming other provisions of the Act did not apply.
(Section 89504.)
For example,
we indicated that under some
circumstances, certain payments for travel and other expenses are
not reportable gifts.
(Section 89506; Regulations 18950.1 and
18950.3.)


Our File No.
I—93—170a
Page
3
In you request for advice,
you clarify that ALEC is a
nonprofit charitable organization.
Thus,
the question you pose in
your letter is whether any travel and lodging given by ALEC is
exempt from the gift limitations because of its status as a
nonprofit charitable organization.
Section 89506 and Regulation 18950.1 provide that a payment
made for travel,
including actual transportation and related
lodging and subsistence, is not subject to the prohibitions or
limitations on honoraria and gifts specified in Sections 89502,
89503,
89504,
or 89505, if:
(1)
The travel is reasonably related to a
legislative or governmental purpose,
or to an issue
of state, national,
or international public policy;
and
(2)
The payment is provided by a government,
a governmental agency,
a foreign government,
a
governmental authority,
a bona fide public or
private educational institution,
defined in Section
203 of the Revenue and Taxation Code,
or by a
nonprofit charitable or religious organization that
is exempt from taxation under Section 501(c) (3)
of
the Internal Revenue Code,
or by a person that is
domiciled outside the United States and that
substantially satisfies the requirements for tax
exempt status under Section 501(c) (3)
of the
Internal Revenue Code.
Therefore, if ALEC is a charitable organization organized
under 501(c) (3)
of the Internal Revenue Code, travel and lodging
provided to the Members of the California Legislature do not
constitute gifts subject to the gift limit if the actual
transportation and related lodging and subsistence is reasonably
related to a legislative or governmental purpose,
or to an issue
of state, national,
or international public policy.
However, the status of the organization as a charitable
organization does not alone exempt travel and lodging provided by
the organization from the gift limitations.
Each expenditure must
be separately analyzed in order to determine if the requirements
of Section 89506 are satisfied.
Moreover, the gifts are subject
to disclosure.
(Section 87207(c).)
You previously stated that the travel is not ordinarily
provided in connection with a speech.
Thus,
we have not further
analyzed the application of Section 89506 and Regulations 18950.1
and 18950.3 in connection to travel where the Member of the
California Legislature is making a speech.
Also, please note that


Our File No.
I-93-170a
Page
4
you have not provided us with any facts which would indicate that
Section 82030(b) (2)
would apply.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter, please
feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—590l.
Sincerely,
Wayne Ordos
Executive Director
fZL c2C%Cc_-’
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel,
Legal Division
cc:
Senator Bill Leonard
3
Section 82030(b) (2)
provides in part that reimbursement, for
travel expenses and per diem received from a bona fide
educational,
academic,
or charitable organization is not “income”
for purposes of the Act where full and adequate consideration is
provided by the official.
(Riddle Advice Letter,
No.
A-89—200;
Ouan Advice Letter,
No.
A-89-182.)
However, the official has the
burden of proving that consideration received by the donor is of
equal or greater value,
and therefore,
not a gift.
(Section
82028.)
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively,
copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0 per page.

or international public policy,
travel and lodging provided to the Members of the California
Legislature do not constitute gifts subject to the ift limits.
